<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">In the event of expected losses on executory contracts, we recognize a liability for the amount that the cost of
settling the contract exceeds the amount of the contract price. When we satisfy these contracts, we derecognize the related liability. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><U>Multiple
Element Arrangements </U></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The main portion of our net sales is derived from contractual arrangements with our customers that have multiple deliverables,
which mainly include the sale of our systems, installation and training services and prepaid extended and enhanced (optic) warranty contracts. As of January&nbsp;1, 2011, we have adopted ASU 2009-13, &#147;Sales Arrangements with Multiple
Deliverables&#148; which amended the guidance on arrangements with multiple deliverables in ASC 605-25. The amended standard changes the requirements for establishing separate units of accounting in a multiple element arrangement and requires the
allocation of arrangement consideration to each deliverable to be based on the relative selling price. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Each element in the arrangement is accounted for
as a separate unit of accounting provided the following criteria are met: the delivered products or services have value to the customer on a standalone basis; and for an arrangement that includes a general right of return relative to the delivered
products or services, delivery or performance of the undelivered product or service is considered probable and is substantially controlled by us. We consider a deliverable to have stand-alone value if the product or service is sold separately by us
or another vendor or could be resold by the customer. Further, our sales arrangements do not include a general right of return relative to the delivered products. Where the aforementioned criteria for a separate unit of accounting are not met, the
deliverable is combined with the undelivered element(s) and treated as a single unit of accounting for the purposes of allocation of the arrangement consideration and revenue recognition. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The hierarchy of evidence to determine a selling price in ASC 605-25 is as follows: </FONT></P>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&#149;</FONT></TD>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">VSOE &#150; the price at which we sell the element in a separate stand-alone transaction; </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&#149;</FONT></TD>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">TPE &#150; evidence from us or other companies of the value of a largely interchangeable element in a transaction; </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&#149;</FONT></TD>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">BESP &#150; our best estimate of the selling price of an element in the transaction. </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">To determine the selling price in multiple elements arrangements, we establish VSOE of the selling price for installation, training services and prepaid extended and enhanced (optic) warranty contracts. VSOE for
installation is determined based on the prices that we charge for billable labor and materials consumed in comparable services (such as relocating a system to another customer site). VSOE for prepaid extended and enhanced (optic) warranty contracts
is determined on the basis of similar products we sell on a standalone basis, such as full service contracts and billable lens swaps, and which are subject to normal price negotiations. Revenue from installation and training services is recognized
when the services are completed. Revenue from prepaid extended and enhanced (optic) warranty contracts is recognized over the term of the contract. When we are unable to establish the selling price using VSOE or TPE, we use BESP. The objective of
using estimated selling price-based methodology is to determine the price at which we would transact a sale if the product or service were sold on a stand-alone basis. Accordingly, we determine BESP considering several internal and external factors
including, but not limited to, pricing practices, gross margin objectives, market conditions, competitive environment, internal costs and geographies. We review selling prices annually and maintain internal controls over the establishment and
updates of these estimates. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><U>Business Combinations </U></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Acquisitions of subsidiaries are included on the basis of the acquisition method. The cost of acquisition is measured based on the consideration transferred at fair
value, the fair value of identifiable assets distributed and the fair value of liabilities incurred or assumed at the acquisition date (i.e., the date at which we obtain control). The excess of the costs of an acquired subsidiary over the net of the
amounts assigned to identifiable assets acquired and liabilities incurred or assumed, is capitalized as goodwill. Acquisition-related costs are expensed when incurred in the period they arise or the service is received. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><U>Evaluation of Long-lived Assets for Impairment </U></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Long-lived
assets include goodwill, other intangible assets and property, plant and equipment. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Goodwill is tested for impairment annually at the end of the third
quarter and whenever events or changes in circumstances indicate that the carrying amount of the goodwill may not be recoverable. These events or circumstances could include a significant change in the business climate, legal factors, operating
performance indicators, competition, or sale or disposition of a significant portion of a reporting unit. The test is based on a two-step approach for each reporting unit (being an operating segment or one level below an operating segment) in which
goodwill has been recorded. First, recoverability is tested by comparing the carrying amount of the reporting unit including goodwill </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P>
<TABLE CELLSPACING="0" CELLPADDING="0" WIDTH="100%" BORDER="0" STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" ALIGN="center">

<TR>
<TD WIDTH="35%"></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" WIDTH="1%"></TD>
<TD WIDTH="32%"></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" WIDTH="1%"></TD>
<TD WIDTH="31%"></TD></TR>
<TR>
<TD VALIGN="top"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">ASML ANNUAL REPORT 2013</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="top" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">27</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="top"></TD></TR></TABLE>


<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">
with the fair value of the reporting unit. If the carrying amount of the reporting unit is higher than the fair value of the reporting unit, the second step should be performed. Goodwill
impairment is measured as the excess of the carrying amount of the goodwill over its implied fair value. The implied fair value of goodwill is determined by calculating the fair value of the various assets and liabilities included in the reporting
unit in the same manner as goodwill is determined in a business combination. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Other intangible assets and property, plant and equipment are reviewed for
impairment whenever events or changes in circumstances indicate that the carrying amount of those assets may not be recoverable. Other intangible assets and property, plant and equipment are tested for impairment based on a two-step approach. First,
the recoverability is tested by comparing the carrying amount of the other intangible assets and property, plant and equipment with their fair value, being the sum of the related undiscounted future cash flows. Second, if the carrying amount of the
other intangible assets and property, plant and equipment is higher than this fair value the assets are considered to be impaired. The impairment to be recognized is measured by the amount by which the carrying amount of the assets exceeds the fair
value of the assets. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">In determining the fair value of a long-lived asset (other than goodwill), we make estimates about future cash flows. These
estimates are based on our financial plan updated with the latest available projection of the semiconductor market conditions and our sales and cost expectations, which are consistent with the plans and estimates that we use to manage our business.
We also make estimates and assumptions concerning WACC and future inflation rates. It is possible that actual results may differ from our plans, estimates and assumptions, which may require impairment of certain long-lived assets (other than
goodwill). Future adverse changes in market conditions may also require impairment of certain long-lived assets (other than goodwill), which could have a material adverse effect on our financial condition and results of operations. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><U>Inventories </U></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Inventories, including spare parts and lenses,
are stated at the lower of cost (first-in, first-out method) or market value. Costs include net prices paid for materials purchased, charges for freight and customs duties, production labor cost and factory overhead. Allowances are made for slow
moving, obsolete or unsellable inventory and are reviewed on a quarterly basis. Our methodology involves matching our on-hand and on-order inventory with our requirements based on the expected demand and resulting manufacturing forecast. In
determining inventory allowances, we evaluate inventory in excess of our forecasted needs on both technological and economic criteria and make appropriate provisions to reflect the risk of obsolescence. This methodology is significantly affected by
our forecasted needs for inventory. If actual requirements were to be lower than estimated, additional inventory allowances for excess or obsolete inventory may be required, which could have a material adverse effect on our business, financial
condition and results of operations. </FONT></P>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><U>Income Taxes </U></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">We operate in various tax jurisdictions in Europe, Asia, and the United States and must comply with the tax laws and regulations of each of these jurisdictions.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">We use the asset and liability method in accounting for income taxes. Under this method, deferred tax assets and liabilities are recognized for tax
consequences attributable to differences between the balance sheet carrying amounts of existing assets and liabilities and their respective tax bases. Furthermore tax assets are recognized for the tax effect of incurred net operating losses. If it
is more likely than not that the carrying amounts of deferred tax assets will not be realized, a valuation allowance is recorded to reduce the carrying amounts of those assets. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">We recognize liabilities for uncertain tax positions based on a two-step process. The first step is to evaluate the tax position for recognition by determining if the weight of available evidence indicates that it
is more likely than not that the position will be sustained on audit, including resolution of related appeals or litigation processes, if any. The second step is to measure the tax benefit as the largest amount that is more than 50 percent likely of
being realized upon settlement. While we believe we have appropriate support for the positions taken on our tax returns, we regularly assess the potential outcomes of examinations by tax authorities in determining the adequacy of our provision for
income taxes, and adjust the income tax provision, income taxes payable and deferred taxes in the period in which the facts that give rise to a revision become known. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><U>Results of Operations 2013 Compared with 2012 </U></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The following discussion and analysis of Results of Operations
should be viewed in the context of the risks that may interfere with our business objectives, described in Item&nbsp;3.D. &#147;Risk Factors&#148;. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Set
out below our Consolidated Statements of Operations data for the years ended December&nbsp;31, 2013 and 2012: </FONT></P> <P STYLE="font-size:12px;margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px">&nbsp;</P>
<P STYLE="line-height:1px;margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:2px;border-bottom:2pt solid #000000">&nbsp;</P>
<TABLE CELLSPACING="0" CELLPADDING="0" WIDTH="100%" BORDER="0" STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" ALIGN="center">


<TR>
<TD WIDTH="80%"></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" WIDTH="5%"></TD>
<TD></TD>
<TD></TD>
<TD></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" WIDTH="5%"></TD>
<TD></TD>
<TD></TD>

<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><B>27.4</B></FONT></TD>

<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">24.8</FONT></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD></TR>
<TR>
<TD VALIGN="top" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">ASP of used system sales</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><B>&nbsp;</B></FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><B>6.9</B></FONT></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><B>&nbsp;&nbsp;</B></FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">7.6</FONT></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD></TR>
</TABLE> <P STYLE="line-height:1px;margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:2px;border-bottom:2pt solid #000000">&nbsp;</P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Net sales increased by EUR
513.8&nbsp;million, or 10.9 percent, to EUR 5,245.3&nbsp;million in 2013 from EUR 4,731.5&nbsp;million in 2012, mainly driven by the increase in net service and field option sales of EUR 322.3&nbsp;million, or 34.7 percent to
EUR&nbsp;1,252.2&nbsp;million in 2013 from EUR 929.9&nbsp;million in 2012. The increase in net service and field option sales is mainly caused by the expansion of Holistic Lithography through increased sales of integrated metrology and feedback loop
technology and by OnPulse revenues as a result of the acquisition of Cymer. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The increase in net system sales of EUR 191.5&nbsp;million, or 5.0 percent,
to EUR 3,993.1&nbsp;million in 2013 from EUR 3,801.6&nbsp;million in 2012 is mainly caused by an increased ASP which more than offsets the lower amount of systems sold. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The increase of the ASP of our systems sold can mainly be explained by the ASP of our new systems sold which increased to EUR 27.4&nbsp;million in 2013 from EUR 24.8&nbsp;million in 2012, which was the result of a
shift in the mix of systems sold towards more high-end system types, NXT:1960Bi and NXT:1970Ci and including one NXE:3300B. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Through 2013, all of the
top 10 chipmakers worldwide, in terms of semiconductor capital expenditure, were our customers. In 2013, recognized sales to our largest customer accounted for EUR 2,058.6&nbsp;million, or 39.2 percent of our net sales. In 2012, recognized sales to
our largest customer accounted for EUR 1,236.1&nbsp;million, or 26.1 percent of our net sales. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Gross profit increased by EUR 172.0&nbsp;million to EUR
2,177.2&nbsp;million or 41.5 percent of net sales in 2013 from EUR 2,005.2&nbsp;million or 42.4 percent of net sales in 2012. Higher gross profit was mainly driven by the increased net system sales. Gross profit as a percentage of net sales in 2013
decreased compared to 2012, mainly due to a 1.6 percent impact from non-cash purchase price accounting adjustments related to the Cymer acquisition. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:24px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><I>Other Income </I></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Other income consists of contributions for
R&amp;D programs under the NRE funding arrangements from Participating Customers of the CCIP and amounted to EUR 64.4&nbsp;million for 2013 (2012:nil). </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:24px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><I>Research and Development Costs </I></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">R&amp;D costs (net of
credits and excluding contributions under the NRE funding agreements from Participating Customers of the CCIP) increased by EUR 292.9&nbsp;million, or 49.7 percent, to EUR 882.0&nbsp;million in 2013 from EUR 589.1&nbsp;million in 2012. R&amp;D
spending mainly increased due the additional investments in EUV source development as a result of the acquisition of Cymer and further investments in our other strategic programs (Immersion and holistic lithography). </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:24px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><I>Selling, General and Administrative Costs </I></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">SG&amp;A costs
increased by EUR 52.4&nbsp;million, or 20.2 percent, to EUR 311.7&nbsp;million in 2013, from EUR 259.3&nbsp;million in 2012. The increase was mainly driven by the acquisition and subsequent integration of Cymer and incurred restructuring expenses.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:24px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><I>Profitability </I></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Our target is to achieve annual
income from operations as percentage of net sales of greater than 20 percent. However in exceptional circumstances, as evidenced by the financial and economic crisis in 2009, we could see periods with income from operations that are substantially
below our minimum target level. </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P>

<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><I>Interest and Other, Net </I></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Interest and other, net increased by EUR 18.2&nbsp;million to EUR 24.4&nbsp;million expense in 2013 from EUR 6.2&nbsp;million expense in 2012. This increase is mainly explained by the loss on the partial
extinguishment of our EUR 600&nbsp;million 5.75 percent senior notes due 2017 and a lower yield generated on our cash and cash equivalents and short-term investments. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:24px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><I>Income Taxes </I></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The effective tax rate increased to 0.8 percent of income before income taxes in 2013 compared to
0.4 percent in 2012. In 2013, the effective tax rate was impacted by the settlement of the favorable pre-existing relationships between Cymer and ASML. This settlement gain is not recognized for tax purposes and was, apart from the R&amp;D tax
incentives, the major driver for the change in the effective tax rate in 2013. In 2012, the effective tax rate was amongst others impacted by a one-time tax benefit resulting from successful conclusion of tax audits. The movement in the liability
for unrecognized tax benefits in 2013 is considered to be limited compared to 2012. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:24px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><I>Net Income </I></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Net income in 2013 amounted to EUR 1,015.5&nbsp;million, or 19.4 percent of net sales, representing EUR 2.36 basic net income per ordinary share, compared with net
income in 2012 of EUR 1,146.3&nbsp;million, or 24.2 percent of net sales, representing EUR 2.70 basic net income per ordinary share. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:24px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><U>Results of
Operations 2012 Compared with 2011 </U></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The following discussion and analysis of Results of Operations should be viewed in the context of the risks that
may interfere with our business objectives, described in Item&nbsp;3.D. &#147;Risk Factors&#148;. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Set out below our Consolidated Statements of
Operations data for the years ended December&nbsp;31, 2012 and 2011: </FONT></P> <P STYLE="font-size:16px;margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px">&nbsp;</P>
<TABLE CELLSPACING="0" CELLPADDING="0" WIDTH="100%" BORDER="0" STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" ALIGN="center">


<TR>
<TD WIDTH="80%"></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" WIDTH="5%"></TD>
<TD></TD>
<TD></TD>
<TD></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" WIDTH="5%"></TD>
<TD></TD>
<TD></TD>
<TD></TD></TR>


<TR>
<TD VALIGN="top" ALIGN="right" STYLE="BORDER-TOP:2px solid #000000"> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px; margin-left:1.00em; text-indent:-1.00em" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><B>Year ended

<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><B>&nbsp;</B></FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><B>7.6</B></FONT></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><B>&nbsp;&nbsp;</B></FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" ALIGN="right"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">3.8</FONT></TD>
<TD NOWRAP VALIGN="bottom"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD></TR>
<TR>
<TD HEIGHT="5"></TD>
<TD HEIGHT="5" COLSPAN="4"></TD>
<TD HEIGHT="5" COLSPAN="4"></TD></TR>
<TR STYLE="font-size:1px">
<TD VALIGN="bottom" STYLE="BORDER-BOTTOM:2px solid #000000">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" STYLE="BORDER-BOTTOM:2px solid #000000">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" STYLE="BORDER-BOTTOM:2px solid #000000">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" STYLE="BORDER-BOTTOM:2px solid #000000">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" STYLE="BORDER-BOTTOM:2px solid #000000">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" STYLE="BORDER-BOTTOM:2px solid #000000">&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" STYLE="BORDER-BOTTOM:2px solid #000000">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" STYLE="BORDER-BOTTOM:2px solid #000000">&nbsp;</TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" STYLE="BORDER-BOTTOM:2px solid #000000">&nbsp;</TD></TR>
</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Net sales decreased by EUR 919.5&nbsp;million, or 16.3 percent, to EUR 4,731.5&nbsp;million in 2012 from EUR 5,651.0&nbsp;million
in 2011. The decrease in net sales mainly resulted from a decrease in net system sales of EUR 1,082.3&nbsp;million, or 22.2 percent, to EUR 3,801.6&nbsp;million in 2012 from EUR 4,883.9&nbsp;million in 2011, partly offset by an increase in net
service and field option sales of EUR 162.8&nbsp;million or 21.2 percent to EUR 929.9&nbsp;million in 2012 from EUR 767.1&nbsp;million in 2011, mainly due the further expansion of Holistic Lithography, integrated metrology and feedback loops. The
number of total systems sold decreased by 23.4 percent to 170 systems in 2012 from 222 systems in 2011, mainly caused by decreased demand in Memory, as its major driver, the PC business shrunk compared to 2011. During 2012, the majority of the
system sales were generated from Logic. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The ASP of our new systems in 2012 EUR 24.8&nbsp;million was in line with 2011 EUR 24.5 million. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">From time to time, we repurchase systems that we have manufactured and sold and, following factory-rebuild or refurbishment, resell those systems to other
customers. This repurchase decision is mainly driven by market demand for capacity expressed by other customers and not by explicit or implicit contractual arrangements relating to the initial sale. The number of used systems sold in 2012 decreased
to 24 from 27 in 2011. The ASP of our used systems increased by 100.0 percent to EUR 7.6&nbsp;million in 2012 from EUR 3.8&nbsp;million in 2011, which was the result of a shift in the mix of used systems sold toward more high-end system types.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Through 2012, all of the top 10 chipmakers worldwide, in terms of semiconductor capital expenditure, were our customers. In 2012, recognized sales to
our largest customer accounted for EUR 1,236.1&nbsp;million, or 26.1 percent of our net sales. In 2011, recognized sales to our largest customer accounted for EUR 1,311.7&nbsp;million, or 23.2 percent of our net sales. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Gross profit decreased to EUR 2,005.2&nbsp;million or 42.4 percent of net sales in 2012 from EUR 2,449.4&nbsp;million or 43.3 percent of net sales in 2011. Lower
gross profit was mainly driven by the decreased number of total systems sold. Gross profit as a percentage of net sales in 2012 decreased compared to 2011, mainly due to increased infrastructure and manufacturing cost, driven primarily by EUV
production, lower utilization of our production capacity and higher cost incurred in relation to excess and obsolete inventory as result of technological developments and design changes. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><I>Research and Development Costs </I></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">R&amp;D costs (net of credits) in 2012 (EUR 589.1 million) were in line with
2011 (EUR 590.3 million). R&amp;D spending remained stable and mainly related to our strategic programs, in particular EUV, immersion and holistic lithography. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><I>Selling, General and Administrative Costs </I></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">SG&amp;A costs increased by EUR 41.4&nbsp;million, or 19.0 percent,
to EUR 259.3&nbsp;million in 2012, or 5.5 percent of net sales, from EUR 217.9&nbsp;million in 2011, or 3.9 percent of net sales. The increase was mainly driven by transaction costs incurred of EUR 26.1&nbsp;million related to the CCIP and
transaction costs related to the proposed acquisition of Cymer and costs to implement and support IT solutions of EUR 10.2 million. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><I>Interest and
Other, Net </I></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Interest and other, net decrease by EUR 13.6&nbsp;million to EUR 6.2&nbsp;million expense in 2012 from EUR 7.4&nbsp;million income in
2011. Interest income relates to interest earned on our cash and cash equivalents and short-term investments; interest income declined in 2012 due to a lower yield earned on cash and cash equivalents and short-term investments, and was more than
offset by the interest expense on our outstanding debt. </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P>

<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><I>Income Taxes </I></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The effective tax rate was 0.4 percent of income before income taxes in 2012, compared with 11.0 percent of income before income taxes in 2011. The change in the
effective tax rate is mainly due to a release of our liability for unrecognized tax benefits of EUR 92.5&nbsp;million after successful conclusion of tax audits in different jurisdictions which almost completely offset the tax expenses, resulting in
an income tax expense of EUR 4.3&nbsp;million (2011: EUR 181.6 million). </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><U>Foreign Exchange Management </U></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">See Item&nbsp;3.D. &#147;Risk Factors &#150; Fluctuations in Foreign Exchange Rates Could Harm Our Results of Operations&#148;, Item&nbsp;11 &#147;Quantitative and
Qualitative Disclosures About Market Risk&#148;, Note 1 and Note 4 to our Financial Statements. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><A NAME="toc546896_18"></A>B. Liquidity and Capital
Resources </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Our cash and cash equivalents increased to EUR 2,330.7&nbsp;million as of December&nbsp;31, 2013 from EUR 1,767.6&nbsp;million as of
December&nbsp;31, 2012 and our short-term investments decreased to EUR 679.9&nbsp;million as of December&nbsp;31, 2013 from EUR 930.0&nbsp;million as of December&nbsp;31, 2012. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">We generated net cash from operating activities of EUR 1,054.2&nbsp;million, EUR 703.5&nbsp;million and EUR 2,070.4&nbsp;million in 2013, 2012 and 2011, respectively. Higher net cash provided by operating
activities in 2013 compared to 2012 relates to increased sales levels and a decrease of our working capital, mainly due to an increase in accounts payable. Lower net cash provided by operating activities in 2012 compared to 2011 relates to decreased
sales levels and decreased accrued and other liabilities mainly as a result of lower amounts of EUV down payments received in 2012. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">We used EUR
368.4&nbsp;million for investing activities in 2013, EUR 1,119.8&nbsp;million in 2012 and EUR 300.9&nbsp;million in 2011. In 2013, our investing activities mainly related to the cash consideration paid for the acquisition of Cymer of EUR
443.7&nbsp;million and the purchase of property, plant and equipment of EUR 210.8&nbsp;million, mainly related to further expansion of our EUV production facilities. This was to a large extent offset by the decrease of short-term investments, mainly
in Dutch Treasury Certificates and deposits with the Dutch government, of EUR 290.2 million. In 2012, our investing activities primarily related to our short-term investments in Dutch Treasury Certificates and deposits with the Dutch government of
EUR 930.0&nbsp;million and purchases of property, plant and equipment of EUR 171.9 million. In 2011, our investing activities mainly related to machinery and equipment, EUV and NXT production facilities in Veldhoven, the Netherlands, information
technology and leasehold improvements to our facilities. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Net cash used in financing activities was EUR 113.1&nbsp;million in 2013, EUR
545.6&nbsp;million in 2012, and EUR 991.6&nbsp;million in 2011. In September 2013, we completed an offering of EUR 750&nbsp;million 3.375 percent senior notes due 2023 with interest payable annually on September&nbsp;19 and, simultaneously, we
repurchased a nominal amount of EUR 361.8&nbsp;million of our EUR 600&nbsp;million 5.75 percent senior notes due 2017 in a tender offer. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">In 2013, net
cash used in financing activities included the net cash proceeds of EUR 740.4&nbsp;million from our notes offering and net cash proceeds of EUR 31.8&nbsp;million from the issuance of shares in connection with the exercise and purchase of employee
stock options, mainly offset by net cash outflows of EUR 368.3&nbsp;million for our notes tender offer and unwinding of related interest rate swaps, EUR 300.0&nbsp;million for our regular share buyback program and EUR 216.1&nbsp;million for our
annual dividend payment. In 2012, net cash used in financing activities includes the net cash outflow of EUR 3,728.3&nbsp;million for the Synthetic Share Buyback, EUR 535.4&nbsp;million for our regular share buyback programs and EUR
188.9&nbsp;million for our annual dividend payment, to a large extent offset by the proceeds of EUR 3,853.9&nbsp;million from issuance of shares under the CCIP and EUR 53.8&nbsp;million net proceeds from issuance of shares in connection with the
exercise and purchase of employee stock options. In 2011, net cash used in financing activities included the cash outflow of EUR 700.5&nbsp;million used in our regular share buyback program, our annual dividend payment of EUR 172.6&nbsp;million and
a repayment of deposits from our customers of EUR 150.0&nbsp;million, partly offset by the net proceeds from issuance of shares in connection with the exercise and purchase of employee stock options of EUR 34.1 million. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Our principal sources of liquidity consist of cash flows from operations, cash and cash equivalents as of December&nbsp;31, 2013 of EUR 2,330.7&nbsp;million,
short-term investments as of December&nbsp;31, 2013 of EUR 679.9&nbsp;million and available credit facilities as of December&nbsp;31, 2013 of EUR 700.0 million. In addition, we may from time to time raise additional capital in debt and equity
markets. Our goal is to remain an investment grade rated company and maintain a capital structure that supports this. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">We invest our cash and cash
equivalents and short-term investments in short-term deposits with financial institutions that have good credit ratings and the Dutch government, in Dutch Treasury Certificates and in AAAm-rated money market funds that invest in high-rated
short-term debt securities of financial institutions and governments. Our investments are predominantly denominated in euros and partly in U.S. dollars. </FONT></P>

<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Our available credit facility consists of an EUR 700.0&nbsp;million committed revolving credit facility from a group
of banks that will mature in 2018. The credit facility contains a restrictive covenant that requires us to maintain a minimum committed capital to net total assets ratio of 40.0 percent calculated in accordance with contractually agreed definitions.
At the end of 2013, we were in compliance with the covenant. Outstanding amounts under this credit facility will bear interest at EURIBOR or LIBOR plus a margin that depends on our liquidity position. No amounts were outstanding under the credit
facility as of the end of 2013. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">We have repayment obligations in 2017, amounting to EUR 238.2&nbsp;million and in 2023, amounting to EUR
750.0&nbsp;million, both relating to our Eurobonds. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">ASML seeks to ensure that cash generated from operations, together with the liquidity provided by
existing cash and cash equivalents and short-term investments and its borrowing capability, will be sufficient to satisfy its liquidity requirements throughout every phase of the industry cycles. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Our liquidity needs are affected by many factors, some of which are based on the normal on-going operations of the business, and others that relate to the
uncertainties of the global economy and the semiconductor industry. Although our cash requirements fluctuate based on the timing and extent of these factors, we believe that cash generated from operations, together with the liquidity provided by
existing cash and cash equivalents and short-term investments and our borrowing capability are sufficient to satisfy our current requirements, including our expected capital expenditures in 2014. We intend to return cash to our shareholders on a
regular basis in the form of dividend payments and, subject to our actual and anticipated liquidity requirements and other relevant factors, share buybacks or capital repayment. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">See Notes 4, 5, 15, 16, 26 and 27 to our Financial Statements for further reference. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><A NAME="toc546896_19"></A>C. Research and Development, Patents and Licenses, etc. </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><U>Research and Development </U></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">See Item&nbsp;4.B. &#147;Business Overview &#150; Research and Development&#148; and
Item&nbsp;5.A. &#147;Operating Results &#150; Results of Operations&#148;. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:14px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1"><U>Intellectual Property Matters </U></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">See Item&nbsp;3.D. &#147;Risk Factors &#150; Failure to Adequately Protect the Intellectual Property Rights Upon Which We depend Could Harm Our Business&#148; and
&#147;Risk Factors &#150; Defending Against Intellectual Property Claims by Others Could Harm Our Business&#148; and Item&nbsp;4.B. &#147;Business Overview &#150; Intellectual Property&#148;. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><A NAME="toc546896_20"></A>D. Trend Information </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">In 2013, our business was supported by continuing momentum in
the Logic segment with customers starting to add capacity at the 20nm node, driven by the mobile applications market. The memory segment continued to pick up in light of tight mobile DRAM capacity. NAND memory bit demand is on a steady growth path
with a stable market outlook, underpinning technology and capacity investments in 2014. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The following table sets forth our systems backlog, excluding
EUV, as of December&nbsp;31, 2013 and 2012. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="font-size:12px;margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px">&nbsp;</P>

recognition dates within 12 months have been assigned. Historically, orders have been subject to cancellation or delay by the customer. Due to possible customer changes in delivery schedules and to cancellation of orders, our systems backlog at any
particular date is not necessarily indicative of actual sales for any succeeding period. </FONT></P>

<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">For the first quarter of 2014, we expect net sales of around EUR 1.4 billion, a gross margin of around 42 percent,
R&amp;D costs of about EUR 280&nbsp;million, other income of about EUR 20&nbsp;million, which consists of contributions from participants of the CCIP and SG&amp;A costs of about EUR 85 million. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The trends discussed in this Item&nbsp;5.D. &#147;Trend information&#148; are subject to risks and uncertainties. See &#147;Part I &#150; Special Note Regarding
Forward Looking Statements&#148; and item 3.D. &#147;Risk Factors&#148;. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><A NAME="toc546896_21"></A>E. Off-Balance Sheet Arrangements
</B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">We have various contractual obligations, some of which are required to be recognized as liabilities in our Financial Statements, including long- and
short-term debt. Other contractual obligations, namely operating lease commitments, purchase obligations and guarantees, are generally not required to be recognized as liabilities on our balance sheet but are required to be disclosed. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><A NAME="toc546896_22"></A>F. Tabular Disclosure of Contractual Obligations </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT

</TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Purchase obligations include purchase commitments with suppliers in the ordinary course of business. ASML expects that it will
honor these purchase obligations to fulfill future sales, in line with the timing of those future sales. The general terms and conditions of the agreements relating to the major part of our purchase commitments as of December&nbsp;31, 2013 contain
clauses that enable us to delay or cancel delivery of ordered goods and services up to the dates specified in the corresponding purchase contracts. These terms and conditions that we typically agree with our supply chain partners gives us additional
flexibility to adapt our purchase obligations to our requirements in light of the inherent cyclicality of the semiconductor equipment industry in which we operate. We establish a provision for cancellation costs when it is probable that the
liability has been incurred and the amount of cancellation fees is reasonably estimable. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><A NAME="toc546896_23"></A>G. Safe Harbor </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">See Part I &#147;Special Note Regarding Forward-Looking Statements&#148;. </FONT></P>

</FONT></TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Mmes. Van den Burg and Van der Meer Mohr and Messrs. Siegle, Westerburgen and Ziebart retired by rotation in 2013. Mr.&nbsp;Siegle and
Mr.&nbsp;Westerburgen retired from the Supervisory Board per the AGM of April&nbsp;24, 2013. In 2013, Ms.&nbsp;Van den Burg was reappointed for a maximum period of two years in line with the Supervisory Board&#146;s profile; Ms.&nbsp;Van der Meer
Mohr and Mr.&nbsp;Ziebart were reappointed for a maximum period of four years in line with the Supervisory Board&#146;s profile. Ms.&nbsp;Smits-Nusteling and Mr.&nbsp;Grose were appointed as member of the Supervisory Board in 2013 for a maximum
period of four years. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Since 2005, the Works Council of ASML Netherlands B.V. has an enhanced right to make recommendations for nomination of one-third
of the members of the Supervisory Board, which recommendations may be rejected by the Supervisory Board in limited circumstances. See Item&nbsp;6.C. &#147;Board Practices &#150; Supervisory Board&#148;. At the 2005 AGM, Ms.&nbsp;Van den Burg was
appointed pursuant to this recommendation right, and at the 2009 AGM and the 2013 AGM she was reappointed in accordance with this recommendation right. At the 2009 AGM, Ms.&nbsp;Van der Meer Mohr was appointed pursuant to this recommendation right,
and at the 2013 General Meeting of Shareholders she was reappointed in accordance with this recommendation right. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">There are no family relationships
among the members of our Supervisory Board and Board of Management. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B>Director and Officer Biographies </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Arthur P.M. van der Poel </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Mr.&nbsp;Van der Poel was
appointed to our Supervisory Board in March 2004 and was appointed as Chairman in 2007. Until 2001, he was the CEO of Philips Semiconductors. Mr.&nbsp;Van der Poel is a former member of the Board of Management (until April 2003) and a former member
of the Group Management Committee of Royal Philips Electronics N.V. Mr. Van der Poel currently serves as a member of the Board of Directors of Gemalto Holding N.V., as a member of the Supervisory Board of Royal HaskoningDHV B.V. and as the chairman
of the Supervisory Board of BDR Thermea. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Fritz W. Fr&ouml;hlich </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Mr.&nbsp;Fr&ouml;hlich was appointed to our Supervisory Board in March 2004. He is the former Deputy Chairman and CFO of Akzo Nobel N.V. Mr.&nbsp;Fr&ouml;hlich is the Chairman of the Supervisory Board of Randstad
Holding N.V. Mr.&nbsp;Fr&ouml;hlich also serves as a member of the Supervisory Boards of Allianz Nederland N.V. and Rexel S.A. and as a member of the Board of Directors of Prysmian Group. </FONT></P>

<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Hendrika (leke) C.J. van den Burg </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Ms.&nbsp;Van den Burg was appointed to our Supervisory Board in March 2005. Ms.&nbsp;Van den Burg was a member of the European Parliament from 1999 until 2009. She was a member of the Supervisory Board of APG Group
N.V. until May&nbsp;15, 2013. Ms.&nbsp;Van den Burg was recently appointed for a new four-year mandate in the Dutch Monitoring Committee Corporate Governance Code. She also serves as chairperson of the Monitoring Foundation Dutch Insurance Companies
(<I>Stichting Toetsing Verzekeraars</I>) and is a member of the Advisory Boards of the Dutch Data Protection Authority (<I>College Bescherming Persoonsgegevens</I>) and Dutch National Register of Supervisory Directors (<I>Nationaal Register
Commissarissen en Toezichthouders</I>). Ms.&nbsp;Van den Burg is also a member of the Advisory Scientific Systemic Committee European Risk Board (ECB Frankfurt) and as a member of the Advisory Council International Affairs Commission Human Rights
(Dutch Ministry Foreign Affairs). </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>OB Bilous </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Mr.&nbsp;Bilous was appointed to our Supervisory Board in March 2005. From 1960 until 2000 Mr.&nbsp;Bilous held various management positions at IBM, including General Manager and Vice President Worldwide
Manufacturing of IBM&#146;s Microelectronics Division. Mr.&nbsp;Bilous also served on the Boards of SMST, ALTIS Semiconductor, Dominion Semiconductor and was chairman of the Board of Sematech from 2000 to 2009. Mr.&nbsp;Bilous currently serves as
Board member of Nantero, Inc. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Pauline F.M. van der Meer Mohr </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Ms.&nbsp;Van der Meer Mohr was appointed to our Supervisory Board in March 2009. Since January&nbsp;1, 2010, Ms.&nbsp;Van der Meer Mohr serves as President of the Executive Board of the Erasmus University
Rotterdam, the Netherlands. Prior to 2010 she was managing partner of the Amstelbridge Group, Senior Executive Vice President at ABN AMRO Bank, Head of Group Human Resources at TNT N.V., and held several senior executive roles at the Royal/Dutch
Shell Group of Companies in various areas. Currently, Ms.&nbsp;Van der Meer Mohr is a member of the Supervisory Boards of Royal DSM N.V. and Duisenberg School of Finance and chairperson of the Executive Board of the Fulbright Center. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Wolfgang H. Ziebart </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Mr.&nbsp;Ziebart was appointed
to our Supervisory Board in March 2009. Mr.&nbsp;Ziebart is the Group Engineering Director of Jaguar Land Rover Ltd. Until May 2008, he was President and CEO of Infineon Technologies A.G. Before Infineon, Mr.&nbsp;Ziebart was on the Boards of
Management of car components manufacturer Continental A.G. and automobile producer BMW A.G. Mr.&nbsp;Ziebart is the chairman of the Supervisory Board of Nordex S.E. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Carla M.S. Smits-Nusteling </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Ms.&nbsp;Smits-Nusteling was appointed to our Supervisory Board in April
2013. Ms.&nbsp;Smits-Nusteling was CFO and member of the Board of Management of Royal KPN N.V. until 2012, and also held several finance and business related positions in Royal KPN N.V. and PostNL. Ms.&nbsp;Smits-Nusteling is a member of the
Supervisory Board of Tele2 AB. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Douglas A. Grose </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Mr.&nbsp;Grose was appointed to our Supervisory Board in April 2013. Mr.&nbsp;Grose was CEO of GlobalFoundries from its inception in 2009 until 2011 and before that, Mr.&nbsp;Grose served as senior vice president
of technology development, manufacturing and supply chain for AMD. Prior to joining AMD in 2007, Mr.&nbsp;Grose spent 25 years at IBM as general manager of technology development and manufacturing for the systems and technology group. Currently,
Mr.&nbsp;Grose is a director of SBA Materials. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Peter T.F.M. Wennink </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Mr.&nbsp;Wennink joined ASML on January&nbsp;1, 1999 and was appointed as Executive Vice President, CFO of ASML and member of our Board of Management on July&nbsp;1, 1999. He was appointed as President and CEO on
July&nbsp;1, 2013. Mr.&nbsp;Wennink has an extensive background in finance and accounting. Prior to his employment with ASML, Mr.&nbsp;Wennink worked as a partner at Deloitte Accountants, specializing in the high technology industry with an emphasis
on the semiconductor equipment industry. Mr.&nbsp;Wennink is a member of the Dutch Institute of Registered Accountants. Mr.&nbsp;Wennink is currently a member of the Supervisory Board of Bank Insinger de Beaufort N.V. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Martin A. van den Brink </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Mr.&nbsp;Van den Brink
joined ASML when the company was founded in 1984. He held several positions in engineering and from 1995 he served as Vice President Technology and member of the Board of Management. Mr.&nbsp;Van den Brink was appointed as Executive Vice President
Product&nbsp;&amp; Technology and member of the Board of Management in 1999. On July&nbsp;1, 2013, Mr.&nbsp;Van den Brink was appointed as President and CTO. Mr.&nbsp;Van den Brink has earned a degree in Electrical Engineering from HTS Arnhem (HAN
University), and a degree in Physics (1984)&nbsp;from the University of Twente, the Netherlands. In 2012, he was awarded an honorary doctorate in physics by the University of Amsterdam, the Netherlands. </FONT></P>

<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Frits J. van Hout </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Mr.&nbsp;Van Hout was appointed as Executive Vice President and Chief Program Officer on July&nbsp;1, 2013. Prior thereto he served as Executive Vice President, CMO and Member of our Board of Management since 2009.
Mr.&nbsp;Van Hout was previously an ASML employee from its founding in 1984 to 1992, in various roles in engineering and sales. From 1998 to 2001, Mr.&nbsp;Van Hout served as CEO of the Beyeler Group, based in the Netherlands and Germany. After
rejoining ASML in 2001, he served as Senior Vice President Customer Support and two Business Units. In 2008, Mr.&nbsp;Van Hout was appointed Executive Vice President Integral Efficiency. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Fr&eacute;d&eacute;ric J.M. Schneider-Maunoury </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Mr.&nbsp;Schneider-Maunoury joined ASML on
December&nbsp;1, 2009, as Executive Vice President and Chief Operations Officer and was appointed to ASML&#146;s Board of Management on March&nbsp;24, 2010. Before joining ASML, Mr.&nbsp;Schneider-Maunoury served as Vice President Thermal Products
Manufacturing of the power generation and rail transport equipment group ALSTOM. Previously, he ran the worldwide Hydro Business of ALSTOM as general manager. Before joining ALSTOM in 1996, Mr.&nbsp;Schneider-Maunoury held various positions at the
French Ministry of Trade and Industry. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Wolfgang U. Nickl </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Mr.&nbsp;Nickl joined ASML on December&nbsp;1, 2013, as Executive Vice President and CFO. He will be appointed as member of ASML&#146;s Board of Management upon the notification to the AGM, scheduled on
April&nbsp;23, 2014. Prior to joining ASML, Mr.&nbsp;Nickl served as Executive Vice President and CFO at Western Digital Corporation, a U.S.-headquartered, NASDAQ-listed developer and manufacturer of storage devices, where he held several financial
and operational leadership roles since joining Western Digital in 1995. Before Western Digital, Mr.&nbsp;Nickl gained experience in finance and IT consulting. He earned a BA in Business from the University of Cooperative Education in Stuttgart,
Germany, and an MBA from the University of Southern California&#146;s Marshall School of Business in Los Angeles, United States. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><A NAME="toc546896_26">
</A>B. Compensation </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The information required by Item&nbsp;6.B. is incorporated by reference from pages 16, 17, 18, 19, 21 and 22 of ASML&#146;s 2013
Remuneration Report which is included as exhibit 99.2 on Form 6-K furnished with the Commission on February&nbsp;12, 2014. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:18px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><A NAME="toc546896_27"></A>C. Board Practices </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B>General
</B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">We endorse the importance of good corporate governance, in which independent supervision, accountability and transparency are the most significant
elements. Within the framework of corporate governance, it is important that a relationship of trust exists between the Board of Management, the Supervisory Board, our employees and our shareholders. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">We pursue a policy of active communication with our shareholders. In addition to the exchange of ideas at the General Meeting of Shareholders, other important
forms of communication include the publication of our annual and quarterly financial results as well as press releases and publications posted on our website. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Our corporate governance structure is intended to: </FONT></P>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&#149;</FONT></TD>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Provide shareholders with regular, reliable, relevant and transparent information regarding our activities, structure, financial condition, performance and other
information, including information on our social, ethical and environmental records and policies; </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&#149;</FONT></TD>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Apply high-quality standards for disclosure, accounting and auditing; and </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&#149;</FONT></TD>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Apply stringent rules with regard to insider securities trading. </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B>Two-Tier Board Structure </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">ASML is incorporated under Dutch law and has a two-tier board structure.
Responsibility for the management of ASML lies with the Board of Management. Independent, non-executive members serve on the Supervisory Board, which supervises and advises the members of the Board of Management in performing their management tasks.
The Board of Management has the duty to keep the Supervisory Board informed, consult with the Supervisory Board on important matters and submit certain important decisions to the Supervisory Board for its approval. The Supervisory Board is
responsible for supervising, monitoring and advising the Board of Management on: (i)&nbsp;the achievement of ASML&#146;s objectives, (ii)&nbsp;the corporate strategy and management of risks inherent to ASML&#146;s business activities, (iii)&nbsp;the
structure and operation of internal risk management and control systems, (iv)&nbsp;the financial reporting process and (v)&nbsp;compliance with applicable legislation and regulations. </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P>
<TABLE CELLSPACING="0" CELLPADDING="0" WIDTH="100%" BORDER="0" STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" ALIGN="center">

<TR>
<TD WIDTH="35%"></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" WIDTH="1%"></TD>
<TD WIDTH="32%"></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom" WIDTH="1%"></TD>
<TD WIDTH="31%"></TD></TR>
<TR>
<TD VALIGN="top"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">ASML ANNUAL REPORT 2013</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="top" ALIGN="center"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">38</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="bottom"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD VALIGN="top"></TD></TR></TABLE>


<p Style='page-break-before:always'>
<HR  SIZE="3" style="COLOR:#999999" WIDTH="100%" ALIGN="CENTER">
<h5 align="left"><a href="#toc">Table of Contents</a></h5>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Supervisory Board members are prohibited from serving as officers or employees of ASML, and members of the Board of
Management cannot serve on the Supervisory Board. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B>Board of Management </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The Board of Management consists of at least two members or such larger number of members as determined by the Supervisory Board. Members of the Board of Management are appointed by the Supervisory Board. The
Supervisory Board must notify the General Meeting of Shareholders of the intended appointment of a member of the Board of Management. As a result of our compliance with the Dutch Corporate Governance Code, members of the Board of Management that are
initially appointed in 2004 or later shall be appointed for a maximum period of four years, but may be re-appointed. Members of the Board of Management serve until the end of the term of their appointment, voluntary retirement, or suspension or
dismissal by the Supervisory Board. In the case of dismissal, the Supervisory Board must first inform the General Meeting of Shareholders of the intended removal. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The Supervisory Board determines the remuneration of the individual members of the Board of Management, in line with the remuneration policy adopted by the General Meeting of Shareholders, upon a proposal of the
Supervisory Board. ASML&#146;s remuneration policy is included in the Remuneration Report. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">For details of the terms of office of the current members of
the Board of Management, see Item&nbsp;6.A &#147;Directors and Senior Management&#148;. Further information relating to the Board of Management required by Item&nbsp;6.C. is incorporated by reference from pages 16, 17, 18 and 19 of ASML&#146;s 2013
Remuneration Report which is included as exhibit 99.2 on Form 6-K, furnished with the Commission on February&nbsp;12, 2014. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B>Supervisory Board
</B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The Supervisory Board consists of at least three members or such larger number as determined by the Supervisory Board. The Supervisory Board
prepares a profile in relation to its size and composition; ASML&#146;s Supervisory Board profile is posted on ASML&#146;s website. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Members of the
Supervisory Board are appointed by the General Meeting of Shareholders from nominations of the Supervisory Board. Nominations must be reasoned and must be made available to the General Meeting of Shareholders and the Works Council simultaneously.
Before the Supervisory Board presents its nominations, both the General Meeting of Shareholders and the Works Council may make recommendations (which the Supervisory Board may reject). In addition, the Works Council has an enhanced right to make
recommendations for nomination of at least one-third of the members of the Supervisory Board, which recommendation may only be rejected by the Supervisory Board: (i)&nbsp;if the relevant person is unsuitable or (ii)&nbsp;if the Supervisory Board
would not be duly composed if the recommended person were appointed as a Supervisory Board member. If no agreement can be reached between the Supervisory Board and the Works Council on these recommendations, the Supervisory Board may request the
Enterprise Chamber of the Amsterdam Court to declare its objection legitimate. Any decision of the Enterprise Chamber on this matter is non-appealable. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Nominations of the Supervisory Board may be rejected by the General Meeting of Shareholders by an absolute majority of the votes representing at least one-third of
the total outstanding capital. If the votes cast in favor of such resolution do not represent at least one-third of the total outstanding capital, a new meeting can be convened at which the nomination can be rejected by an absolute majority. If a
nomination is rejected, the Supervisory Board must make a new nomination. If a nomination is not rejected and the General Meeting of Shareholders does not appoint the nominated person, the Supervisory Board will appoint the nominated person.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Members of the Supervisory Board serve for a maximum term of four years from the date of their appointment, or a shorter period as set out in the
rotation schedule as adopted by the Supervisory Board. They may be re-appointed, provided that their entire term of office does not exceed twelve years. The General Meeting of Shareholders may, with an absolute majority of the votes representing at
least one-third of the total outstanding capital, dismiss the Supervisory Board in its entirety for lack of confidence. In such event, the Enterprise Chamber of the Amsterdam Court shall appoint one or more members of the Supervisory Board at the
request of the Board of Management. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Upon the proposal of the Supervisory Board, the General Meeting of Shareholders determines the remuneration of the
members of the Supervisory Board. A member of the Supervisory Board may not be granted any shares or option rights by way of remuneration. </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P>

 <P STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">For details of the terms of office of the current members of the Supervisory Board, see Item&nbsp;6.A &#147;Directors
and Senior Management&#148;. Further information relating to the Supervisory Board required by Item&nbsp;6.C. is incorporated by reference from pages 21 and 22 of ASML&#146;s 2013 Remuneration Report which is included as exhibit 99.2 on Form 6-K,
furnished with the Commission on February&nbsp;12, 2014. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B>Approval of Board of Management Decisions </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The Board of Management requires prior approval of the General Meeting of Shareholders for resolutions concerning an important change in the identity or character
of ASML or its business, including: </FONT></P>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&#149;</FONT></TD>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">A transfer of all or substantially all of the business of ASML to a third party; </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&#149;</FONT></TD>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Entering into or the termination of a long-term material joint venture between ASML and a third party; and </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE>
<TABLE STYLE="BORDER-COLLAPSE:COLLAPSE" BORDER="0" CELLPADDING="0" CELLSPACING="0" WIDTH="100%">
<TR>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top" ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">&#149;</FONT></TD>
<TD WIDTH="1%" VALIGN="top"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></TD>
<TD ALIGN="left" VALIGN="top"> <P ALIGN="left"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">An acquisition or divestment by ASML of an interest in the capital of a company with a value of at least one-third of ASML&#146;s assets (determined by reference
to ASML&#146;s most recently adopted Statutory Annual Report). </FONT></P></TD></TR></TABLE> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B>Rules of Procedure </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The Board of Management and the Supervisory Board have adopted Rules of Procedure for each of the Board of Management, Supervisory Board and the four Committees of
the Supervisory Board. These Rules of Procedure are posted on our website. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B>Directors and Officers Insurance and Indemnification </B></FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">Members of the Board of Management and Supervisory Board, as well as certain senior management members, are insured under ASML&#146;s Directors and Officers
Insurance Policy. Although the insurance policy provides for a wide coverage, our directors and officers may incur uninsured liabilities. ASML has agreed to indemnify its Board of Management and Supervisory Board against any claims arising in
connection with their position as director and officer of ASML, provided that such claim is not attributable to willful misconduct or intentional recklessness of such officer or director. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT
STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B>Corporate Governance Developments </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">ASML continuously monitors and assesses applicable corporate governance
rules, including recommendations and initiatives regarding principles of corporate governance. These include rules that have been promulgated in the United States both by NASDAQ and by the SEC. See also Item&nbsp;16.G. &#147;Corporate
Governance&#148;. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The Dutch Corporate Governance Code came into effect on January&nbsp;1, 2004 and was amended as of January&nbsp;1, 2009. Dutch listed
companies are required to either comply with the principles and the best practice provisions of the Code, or to explain on which points they deviate from these best practice provisions and why. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">ASML reports on its compliance with the Dutch Corporate Governance Code in its Statutory Annual Report for the year ended December&nbsp;31, 2013. </FONT></P>
<P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B>Committees of ASML&#146;s Supervisory Board </B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">While
retaining overall responsibility, the Supervisory Board assigns certain of its tasks to its four committees: the Audit Committee, the Remuneration Committee, the Selection and Nomination Committee and the Technology and Strategy Committee. Members
of these committees are appointed from among the Supervisory Board members. </FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">The chairman of each committee reports to the Supervisory Board verbally
and when deemed necessary in writing, the issues and items discussed in each meeting. In addition, the minutes of each committee are available to all members of the Supervisory Board, enabling the Supervisory Board to make the appropriate decisions.
</FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="2"><B><I>Audit Committee </I></B></FONT></P> <P STYLE="margin-top:12px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT STYLE="font-family:ARIAL" SIZE="1">ASML&#146;s Audit
Committee is composed of three members of the Supervisory Board. The current members of our Audit Committee are Fritz Fr&ouml;hlich (chairman), Arthur van der Poel and Carla Smits-Nusteling, each of whom is an independent, non-executive member of
our Supervisory Board. The Supervisory Board has determined that Fritz Fr&ouml;hlich qualifies as the Audit Committee financial expert pursuant to Section&nbsp;407 of the Sarbanes-Oxley Act of 2002 and the rules promulgated thereunder. Our external
auditor, our CEO, our CFO, our Corporate Controller, our Corporate Chief Accountant, our Senior Director Corporate Risk and Assurance (Internal Audit), as well as other ASML employees invited by the chairman of the Audit Committee may also attend
the meetings of the Audit Committee. </FONT></P>
 <p STYLE="margin-top:0px;margin-bottom:0px"><FONT SIZE="1">&nbsp;</FONT></P>
